frameworks.name: chain-of-thought reasoning
frameworks.one_line: extended reasoning as the key driver of human-like decision behaviour in LLMs

frameworks.name: active probabilistic reasoning
frameworks.one_line: task framework separating evidence sampling from belief inference

frameworks.name: near-optimal reference policy
frameworks.one_line: Bayes-optimal benchmark for sampling and inference

frameworks.name: latent-variable mechanistic model
frameworks.one_line: four interpretable latents (memory, strategy, choice bias, occlusion awareness) place agents in shared cognitive space

core_question: Do language models decide under uncertainty like humans, and what role does chain-of-thought reasoning play in that process?
key_assumptions: sampling and inference are dissociable sub-processes of decision-making under uncertainty
deviations from optimal behaviour can be captured by a small set of interpretable latent variables
humans and LLMs can be embedded in a shared low-dimensional cognitive space
manipulations: presence and extent of chain-of-thought reasoning across LLMs
agent type (humans vs broad set of contemporary LLMs)
active sampling vs inference phase of the task
key_findings: extended reasoning produces large gains in inference but only modest gains in active sampling
with CoT, LLM belief trajectories become strikingly human-like
latent-variable model reproduces behavioural signatures across humans and LLMs in a shared space
a persistent gap between humans and LLMs remains in information acquisition
interpretations: chain-of-thought shifts LLMs toward human-like evidence accumulation and belief-to-choice mapping
alignment to human cognition tightens in inference but not in active sampling
limitations_noted: persistent human-LLM gap in active information acquisition not closed by CoT
